dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Medic 68 for traumatic injury with suspected left forearm deformity at [STREET_ADDRESS].
Hi, I'm Medic 68. A 20-year-old male fell during soccer and is reporting a possible forearm injury.
He's experiencing a 10/10 sharp pain in his left forearm after landing on it during a fall.
His eyes opened to verbal stimuli and are following commands.
Verbal responses are oriented; he knows people and where he is.
He can move all extremities and follows commands appropriately.
He's alert and oriented to person, place, and time-AOx4 with a GCS of 15.
Airway is patent and clear with no signs of obstruction.
Observing adequate and non-labored breathing at 20 per minute.
Peripheral pulses are present, and there's no sign of active bleeding.
His SpO2 is 96%, 98%, and 97% on room air, indicating no hypoxemia.
The patient states pain began 10 minutes ago after a fall while playing soccer.
No known drug allergies reported by the patient.
Patient denies any current medication use.
No significant past medical history reported.
No information provided on last oral intake.
During soccer, he fell and landed on his left arm, causing the pain.
The onset of sharp pain was ten minutes ago after landing on the arm.
Movement of the arm increases the localized pain significantly.
Describes the pain as sharp and nonradiating.
The pain does not radiate to other areas of the body.
He rates it as 10/10.
Has been present for about 10 minutes now.
HEENT findings unremarkable with no trauma or DCAP-BTLS.
Pupils are equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation, approx. 4 mm.
No deformity in the neck or back observed and no reported pain.
Chest is unremarkable and without deformities or trauma.
Symmetrical and clear bilateral breath sounds with equal chest rise.
No tracheal deviation or jugular venous distention is noted.
Soft, nontender abdomen with active bowel sounds.
No complaint or palpable deformity to the pelvis.
Noted deformation at the left forearm proximal to the wrist. PMS intact.
Skin is warm and dry, with no rashes or injuries.
Pulse measured at 84, 170, then dropped to 62 without apparent cause.
Respiratory rate remains at 20 throughout the initial assessment.
Blood pressure recorded at 106/68, 114/59, and 113/55 respectively.
Glucose levels were not measurable using the device.
SpO2 was noted as 96%, 98%, and then 97% without need for supplemental oxygen.
EKG showed sinus rhythm with 4-lead cardiac monitoring.
30mg Ketorolac IM administered. Response described as unchanged.
100mcg Fentanyl IV with 10 mL NS and 4mg Ondansetron IV follow with improved patient response.
18G IV initiated in the right wrist vein in response to protocol.
Continuous ECG monitoring is in place using four leads.
Left forearm was splinted to immobilize the area.
No facial droop noted during examination.
Speech is clear with no signs of slurring noted.
All treatments were given under the general-pain control protocol.
Transporting non-urgent to Parham unless a change in condition occurs.
Patient is ambulatory but is now safely secured on the stretcher.
Reassessed vitals showed stable readings throughout transport.
ECG monitoring continued, with no additional interventions needed.
Pain remained at 10/10 until Fentanyl administration, then decreased.
EKG consistent in sinus rhythm with rewired monitoring during transport.
Patient, stretcher, and ambulance secured for transport using safety belts.
Reflexes and movement of the neck and back are unremarkable.
No pulsating masses detected in the abdomen.
Patient denies nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.
Continuous venous assessments ongoing as requested by protocol.
Safety belts on the stretcher are engaged to transport the patient securely.
Pupils remain reactive to light throughout assessment and reassessment.
The stretcher is now fully secured inside the ambulance.
Vitals remain stable and within manageable limits during transport.
No abdominal masses detected during repeated assessments.
Toradol showed no change in patient's condition as noted.
No signs of airway obstruction, breathing or circulation changes noted.
No significant changes have occurred during the transport phase.
The ambulance is ready for departure and patient is stable.
No signs of facial droop or slurred speech throughout the assessment.
Transporting without lights or sirens to Parham as per protocol.
Recordings are documented and time-stamped correctly in transport protocol.
Departing the scene with smooth handover and secure placement.
No signs of rib injuries or crepitus on physical assessment.
No spill or unsecured movement seconds after entering the ambulance.
Reassuring signs of stability with Fentanyl showing benefit.
No palpable lymphadenopathy or neck masses are seen.
IV remains secure and connected throughout movement and transport.
Oxygen use on board remains unnecessary during this shift in state.
Interventions are classified as completed before transport decision.
Patient denies headache, vision changes, or other neurological signs.
EKG and vital signs on monitor are stable with no signals of distress.
Pulse has decreased from maximum spikes but no flutter or aberrancy with NSR.
Assessing signs of aging and impact to current medical condition in the patient.
Team remains reliable and performing as trained for prehospital transport.
Reassessments will be conducted periodically per routine for any change.
Oxygen saturation levels remained within stable percentage thresholds.
Patient appears more comfortable after receiving first dose of pain medication.
Head signs unremarkable and repeat assessments show no change.
Patient under instruction but appears to understand transport process.
All action and changes are well-timed and documented in field record book.
All administered medications are documented in real-time.
Upon departure from the scene, ambient procedures are done appropriately.
IV site is dry, intact, and functioning without pain or significant swelling.
Ambulance and all procedures are ready for transport phase at this point.
Skin temperature and color reported as consistent with initial assessment.
No signs or signals of collapse or deteriorating rhythms pre-trip.
Scene is left clean and no impediments are left behind after exodus.
Secure interests in the patient's condition remain consistent past this juncture.
All steps within monitoring protocol completed to standard.
Rechecking all primary vitals to situate the transport parameters properly.
Patient is shipped stable and brought to trauma unit without incident.
The medical team remains ready for subsequent reassessments if need be.
All parties are aware of patient's condition and status at the time of departure.
All supporting factors for transport have reached high-grade confirmation.
Weather conditions and outside parameters are neutral to transport process.
Transport route is commenced with intentions of timely arrival at next phase.
Completion of successful transport with patient arrival for study and further processing.
Prehandover assessment complete upon arrival at transport destination.
All causation assumptions of major symptoms are cleared for medical handoff.
Arrival of patient at designated receiving location intact and observational.
Full documentation completed to satisfaction of all acting parties.
Team and ambulance are off-duty pending further assignment for this transport call.
Transport concluded with final report logistics being addressed at end destination.
All passive data from transport monitoring systems are also at disposal for healthcare team.
Team awaits handover decision and confirmation as part of protocol process.
No incidence reported on the street to affect this transport in any degree.
Respiratory functions remain stable while in transport condition.
No pre-monitory signs of distress marked during travel with patient.
